author: draganrakita
content: It is not the same, values inside EIP-8038 is about state access that are first encountered in execution for transaction all clients do a lot of pre block loading and preparing so its work is compensated

author: draganrakita
content: 12k value iirc is taken from benchmarking (that makes sense to me) cc <@968526635735326741> for confirmation.

author: misilva73
content: Yes, I replied in the PR. <@508125616940515329> , read the rationale section for more info on how the values are set

author: draganrakita
content: <@1361003005691822160> i think this should explain things around TX_BASE pricing

author: gaxanh
content: I agree that those values ​​are derived from measurements, but I think we should also have a reasonable justification based on the pricing model we have to justify the observed values, and I have provided a relatively suitable explanation (in essence, `TX_VALUE_COST` is **half of** `ACCOUNT_WRITE`) with the given pricing model.

author: draganrakita
content: But those are not the same, and will never be same, a lot of pre block work is done in clients that compansates the cpu

author: draganrakita
content:
> have a reasonable justification based on the pricing model we have to justify the observed values
We dont need it, benchmarks shown the data for tx transfer, in same way how `ACCOUNT_WRITE` is defined for frame.

author: gaxanh
content:
So, is it correct to say that the similarity in the values I observed is merely coincidental?

I also wanted to clarify the semantics of `ACCOUNT_WRITE`. Is it intended to represent the cost of updating both the caller and the callee as a single unit? If so, does that imply that the cost of account state transitions is identical across all execution paths?

For example, suppose account A (cold write) transfers Ether to account B (cold write), updating both A and B. Later in the same transaction, A (warm write) transfers Ether to account C (cold write). Would both operations still incur the same `ACCOUNT_WRITE` cost, regardless of the warm/cold status of the individual account writes?

author: draganrakita
content:
> So, is it correct to say that the similarity in the values I observed is merely coincidental?
Would say yes, for example all tx sig recoveries are done in parallel, so cost is a lot lower, and it is hard to measure. TX_BASE is good guess done in benchmark.

author: draganrakita
content:
> I also wanted to clarify the semantics of ACCOUNT_WRITE. Is it intended to represent the cost of updating both the caller and the callee as a single unit? If so, does that imply that the cost of account state transitions is identical across all execution paths?
This is not taken into account when doing calculations, we dont check if target has already changed or not. This is something that got proposed in saparate EIP but was not included in Glamsterdam.

author: gaxanh
content: I see now, ERC20 is being favored over ETH 🤣

author: draganrakita
content:
> I see now, ERC20 is being favored over ETH 🤣
This could be true in terms of gas. Charles proposed to do EIP-2200 but for accounts, it makes a lot of sense imo, but it is complex and in the end just a gas optimization.

author: gaxanh
content: I don't think that's correct. Optimizing gas usage is effectively a legitimate way to increase block throughput, so the benefits are far from negligible.

content: btw <@1361003005691822160> what team are you on?

author: gaxanh
content: I'm just an individual developer who wants to contribute to building EIPs 🙏

author: misilva73
content:
> I also wanted to clarify the semantics of ACCOUNT_WRITE. Is it intended to represent the cost of updating both the caller and the callee as a single unit?
I replied to this already, <@1361003005691822160>. `ACCOUNT_WRITE` is a single account write. The callee cost is part of `TX_BASE_COST`
